To connect the regulator to the LP tank, follow these steps:1.  Ensure the LP tank is properly secured onto the grill.2.  Turn all control knobs to the OFF position.3.  Turn the LP tank OFF by turning the OPD hand wheel clockwise to a full stop.4.  Remove the protective cap from the LP tank valve. Always use the cap and strap supplied with the valve.<PIC>Do not use a POL transport plug (plastic part with external threads), as it will defeat the safety feature of the valve.<PIC>5.  Hold the regulator and insert the nipple into the LP tank valve. Hand-tighten the coupling nut, holding the regulator in a straight line with the LP tank valve to avoid cross-threading. The nipple has to be centered into the LP tank valve.<PIC>6.  Turn the coupling nut clockwise and tighten to a full stop. The regulator will seal on the back-check feature in the LP tank valve, resulting in some resistance. An additional one-half to three-quarters turn is required to complete the connection. Tighten by hand only—do not use tools.

To ensure your safety, perform a leak test before each use. Follow these steps:1.  Turn all grill control knobs to the OFF position.2.  Make sure the regulator is tightly connected to the LP tank.3.  Completely open the LP tank valve by turning the OPD hand wheel counterclockwise. If you hear a rushing sound, turn the gas off immediately, as this indicates a major leak at the connection.4.  Brush a soapy solution onto the areas indicated in the picture below.      <PIC>5.  Check for leaks. If "growing" bubbles appear, there is a leak. Immediately close the LP tank valve and retighten the connections. If the leaks cannot be stopped, do not try to repair it.6.  Always close the LP tank valve after performing the leak test by turning the hand wheel clockwise.      <PIC>Additional safety checks and reminders:- **Hose Check:** Before each use, check to see if hoses are cut, worn, or kinked. Replace damaged hoses before using the grill.      <PIC>- **Valve Check:** Make sure the gas is off at the LP tank before checking valves. Push in the knobs and release; they should spring back. If they do not, replace the valve assembly. Turn the knobs to the LO position and then back to OFF; they should turn smoothly.- Do not use the grill until it has been leak-tested.- If a leak is detected at any time, STOP and call the fire department.- If you cannot stop a gas leak, immediately close the LP tank valve and call your LP gas supplier or fire department.

When using the grill for indirect cooking, here are the key points to pay attention to:**Always Cook with the Lid Closed**Due to weather conditions, cooking times may vary. During cold and windy conditions, the temperature setting may need to be increased to ensure sufficient cooking temperatures.<PIC>**How Indirect Cooking Works**Poultry and large cuts of meat cook slowly to perfection on the grill by indirect heat. The heat from selected burners circulates gently throughout the grill, cooking meat or poultry without the touch of a direct flame. This method greatly reduces flare-ups when cooking extra fatty cuts because there is no direct flame to ignite the fats and juices that drip during cooking.<PIC>**Burner Configuration**You can use 1-Burner Cooking with direct or indirect heat. This is best for smaller meals or foods and consumes less fuel.**Food Safety**Follow these four basic steps to keep food safe from harmful bacteria:*   **Clean:** Wash hands, utensils, and surfaces with hot soapy water before and after handling raw meat and poultry.*   **Separate:** Separate raw meats and poultry from ready-to-eat foods to avoid cross contamination. Use a clean platter and utensils when removing cooked foods.*   **Cook:** Cook meat and poultry thoroughly to kill bacteria. Use a thermometer to ensure proper internal food temperatures.*   **Chill:** Refrigerate prepared foods and leftovers promptly.For more information, you can call the USDA Meat and Poultry Hotline at 1-800-535-4555 (In Washington, DC (202) 720-3333, 10:00 am-4:00 pm EST).

根据手册，组装过程的前三个步骤如下：1.  需要两个人将烧烤炉头抬起并放置到推车上。小心地将烧烤炉头降低到推车上，将烧烤炉头底部的槽口与推车侧板上的立柱对齐。确保调节器软管悬挂在推车内部。    <PIC>2.  首先，拆下侧燃烧器阀门支架上出厂时安装的两个螺丝和锁紧垫圈。将侧燃烧器阀门支架放置在侧燃烧器搁板下方，使阀杆穿过搁板中央较大的孔。将阀门支架上的孔与搁板上的左右孔对齐。使用从支架上拆下的锁紧垫圈和螺丝进行固定。3.  使用四个螺丝将灯适配器安装到后面板上。

Here are some key safety tips for using your grill:*   **Before Starting:** Check that the coupling nut is tight before opening the LP tank valve.*   **During Operation:** Never move the grill while it is in operation or still hot. Use long-handled barbecue utensils and oven mitts to avoid burns and splatters.*   **Load Limits:** The maximum load for the side shelves is 10 lbs. Do not use a cooking pot larger than 9 inches in diameter on the grid.*   **Grease Management:** The grease tray must be installed into the opening in the back panel and should be emptied after each use. Do not remove the grease tray until the grill has completely cooled.*   **If You Notice a Drip:** If grease or other hot material drips from the grill onto the valve, hose, or regulator, turn off the gas supply at once. Determine the cause, correct it, then clean and inspect the valve, hose, and regulator before continuing. Perform a leak test.*   **When Not in Use:** Turn off all control knobs and the LP tank valve.*   **Regulator Noises:** The regulator may make a humming or whistling noise during operation. This will not affect the safety or use of the grill.*   **Regulator Frost:** If the regulator frosts, turn off the grill and LP tank valve immediately. This indicates a problem with the tank, and it should not be used on any product—return it to the supplier!    <PIC>*   **Burner Won't Light:** If the burner does not light, turn the knobs to OFF, wait 5 minutes, and try again. Always close the valve during the 5-minute waiting period. If the burner does not ignite with the valve open, gas will continue to flow out of the burner and could accidentally ignite with risk of injury.    <PIC>

The identification numbers on your jetski are used to identify your specific watercraft and engine, which is essential for ordering parts or in case of theft. There are three main numbers you should record:1.  **Primary Identification (PRI-ID) Number**    This number is stamped on a label attached inside the engine compartment.    <PIC>    <PIC>2.  **Hull Identification Number (HIN)**    The HIN is stamped on a plate attached to the hull on the aft, starboard (right) side.    <PIC>    <PIC>3.  **Engine Serial Number**    The engine serial number is stamped on a label attached to the engine unit.    <PIC>    <PIC>You should record these numbers and keep them in a separate place in case your watercraft is stolen.

Here are the key cruising limitations you should follow for safe operation:*   **Scan constantly** for people, objects, and other watercraft. Be alert for conditions that limit your visibility or block your vision of others.    <PIC>*   **Operate defensively** at safe speeds and keep a safe distance away from people, objects, and other watercraft.*   **Do not follow** directly behind other watercraft or boats.*   **Avoid spraying or splashing** others; do not go near them for this purpose.*   **Avoid sharp turns** or other maneuvers that make it hard for others to avoid you or understand where you are going.*   **Avoid areas** with submerged objects or shallow water.*   **Ride within your limits** and avoid aggressive maneuvers to reduce the risk of loss of control, ejection, and collision.*   **Do not operate in rough water, bad weather, or poor visibility.** Be alert to the possibility of adverse weather and check forecasts before setting out.    <PIC>*   **Never operate in water less than 60 cm (2 ft) deep**, as this increases your chance of hitting a submerged object, which could result in injury.*   **Do not jump wakes or waves.** This is a high-performance boat, and such actions can increase the risk of back/spinal injury, facial injuries, and broken bones.    <PIC>*   **Do not operate after sunset or before dawn.** This watercraft is not equipped with lighting required for night operation, which increases the risk of collision.Also, remember that you should not ride without someone else nearby. If you ride further than swimming distance from shore, you should be accompanied by another boat or watercraft, but always maintain a safe distance.

Here are the key cruising limitations you should follow when operating your watercraft:- **Scan constantly** for people, objects, and other watercraft. Be alert for conditions that limit your visibility or block your vision of others.  <PIC>- **Operate defensively** at safe speeds and keep a safe distance away from people, objects, and other watercraft.  - **Do not follow** directly behind watercraft or other boats.  - **Do not go near** others to spray or splash them with water.  - **Avoid sharp turns** or other maneuvers that make it hard for others to avoid you or understand where you are going.  - **Avoid areas** with submerged objects or shallow water.  - **Ride within your limits** and avoid aggressive maneuvers to reduce the risk of loss of control, ejection, and collision.  - **Do not operate** in rough water, bad weather, or when visibility is poor; this may lead to an accident causing injury or death. Be alert to the possibility of adverse weather and check forecasts and prevailing conditions before setting out.  <PIC>- **Never operate** in water that is less than 60 cm (2 ft) deep, otherwise you increase your chance of hitting a submerged object, which could result in injury.  - **Do not jump wakes or waves.** Sharp turns or jumping wakes or waves can increase the risk of back/spinal injury (paralysis), facial injuries, and broken legs, ankles, and other bones.  <PIC>- **Do not operate** after sunset or before dawn. This watercraft is not equipped with lighting required for night operation, which increases the risk of collision.Additionally, always remember that watercraft and other boats do not have brakes. Do not release the throttle when trying to steer away from objects—you need throttle to steer.

Before operating your watercraft, you must complete these key requirements:1. **Read the provided materials** – Thoroughly read the Owner’s/Operator’s Manual, the Riding Practice Guide, the Riding Instruction card, and all warning and caution labels on the watercraft.2. **Watch the Basic Orientation Video** – This video is provided with your watercraft and helps reinforce the information in the printed materials.3. **Never allow untrained operators** – Do not let anyone else operate the watercraft until they have also read all the manuals, the practice guide, the instruction card, and all warning labels, and if possible, watched the Basic Orientation Video.4. **Secure storage compartments** – Make sure all storage compartments are properly secured before riding. Note that they are not designed to be waterproof, so place items that must stay dry (like the manuals) in a waterproof bag.5. **Carry the manual and tool kit** – It is advisable to always have the Owner’s/Operator’s Manual and tool kit with you. A dedicated storage compartment is provided on the watercraft for this purpose.  <PIC>  <PIC>

Your jetski has several key characteristics:- **Jet thrust steering**: The watercraft is water-jet propelled, and jet thrust turns it. If you release the throttle completely, you only get minimum thrust, and at speeds above trolling, your ability to steer without throttle decreases rapidly. You may still have some turning ability right after releasing the throttle, but once the engine slows down, the watercraft won't respond to handlebar input until you open the throttle again or reach trolling speed.  <PIC>- **No neutral position**: The jet pump is directly connected to the engine, so jet thrust produces some movement whenever the engine is running. There is no "neutral" position.  <PIC>- **Intake and nozzle hazards**: Keep away from the intake grate while the engine is on — long hair, loose clothing, or PFD straps can get entangled in moving parts, causing severe injury or drowning. Never insert any object into the jet thrust nozzle while the engine is running, as contact with the rotating parts of the jet pump could result in severe injury or death.  <PIC>

When you're operating your jetski (which is a power-driven vessel), you must follow the **Steering and Sailing Rules** whenever you encounter other vessels. These rules determine who is the “stand-on” vessel (has right-of-way) and who is the “give-way” vessel (must yield).  The three main situations you may encounter that could lead to a collision are:  1. **Meeting** – you are approaching another vessel head-on  2. **Crossing** – you are traveling across another vessel’s path  3. **Overtaking** – you are passing or being passed by another vessel  In the illustration below, your watercraft is in the center. You should give the right-of-way to any vessels shown in the white area (you are the give-way vessel). Any vessels in the shaded area must yield to you (they are the give-way vessels). Both you and the meeting vessel must alter course to avoid each other.  <PIC>  **If you are the give-way vessel:**  - Take positive and timely action to stay out of the way of the stand-on vessel.  - Normally, do not cross in front of the vessel with the right-of-way.  - Slow down or change directions briefly and pass behind the other vessel.  - Always move in such a way that the operator of the other vessel can see what you are doing.  **Additional right-of-way rules to remember:**  - **Sailing vessels** should normally be given the right-of-way, except when the sailing vessel is overtaking you, in which case you have the right-of-way.  - **Fishing vessels** (those fishing with nets, lines, or trawls) have the right-of-way regardless of position. Vessels with trolling lines are not considered fishing vessels.  - In a **narrow channel**, a sailing vessel should not hamper your safe passage if you can navigate only in such a channel.  **General Prudential Rule:** If a collision appears unavoidable, neither boat has the right-of-way — both must take action to avoid the collision.

You have two main engine switches to operate on this jetski:1.  **To start the engine:** Push the start switch (the green button).      <PIC>2.  **To stop the engine normally:** Push the engine stop switch (the red button).      <PIC>**Important safety notes to keep in mind:***   The engine will not start if the clip is removed from the engine shut-off switch.*   Do not run the engine at full throttle for more than 15 seconds when checking the meter on land, as the engine could overheat.

根据手册内容，摩托艇上有两种操纵杆，分别是**油门杆**和**阻风门杆**，它们的操作方法如下：1.  **油门杆**    *   **加速**：握紧油门杆 $\textcircled{1}$ 即可提高发动机转速。    *   **减速或怠速**：松开油门杆，发动机转速会降低并回到怠速位置。    <PIC>2.  **阻风门杆**    *   **打开阻风门**：冷机启动时，需要更浓的油气混合比。将阻风门杆 $\textcircled{1}$ 向 $\circledast$ 方向拨动。    *   **关闭阻风门**：将阻风门杆向 $\circledcirc$ 方向拨回。    <PIC>

The Quick Shift Trim System (QSTS) selector is located on the left handlebar grip and is used to adjust the trim angle of your watercraft. It works by changing the angle of the jet thrust nozzle vertically, which in turn alters the trim angle of the watercraft.The selector has 5 positions: 2 bow down positions, a neutral “N” position, and 2 bow up positions.**To change the trim angle:**1.  Reduce engine speed to 3,000 r/min or less.2.  Squeeze the shift lock lever, and then turn the QSTS selector to the desired position.3.  Release the shift lock lever to lock the QSTS selector.<PIC>**Important cautions and notes:***   Do not turn the QSTS selector while operating the watercraft at full throttle, as damage could occur to the QSTS.*   The neutral “N” position will provide good performance for most operating conditions. To enhance particular types of performance, select a bow down or bow up position.<PIC>

The fuel meter shows the remaining fuel level, and the hour meter shows the total engine operation time.  **Fuel meter**  The fuel meter has eight segments that indicate how much fuel is left in the tank. Use it as a reference, because the segment reading can differ depending on your operating condition.  <PIC>  **Hour meter**  The hour meter displays the hours of engine operation that have elapsed since the watercraft was new. This helps you follow the maintenance schedule.  <PIC>  Note: The elapsed time is kept even if the battery terminals are disconnected. If the hour meter malfunctions, "$-\_-$" and the warning light will blink.

Inspecting the fuel filter and fuel tank on your watercraft is important, but the inspection and replacement of the fuel filter must be handled by a dealer due to safety risks. Here is how to proceed:### Fuel Filter Inspection & ReplacementYou should not try to inspect or replace the fuel filter yourself. An incorrectly installed filter can leak gasoline, which could lead to fire or explosion. The filter is a one-piece, disposable unit.<PIC>*   **Replacement Interval**: The fuel filter should be replaced after the initial 10 hours or first month of operation, and every 200 hours or 24 months thereafter. It should also be replaced if water is found in the filter.*   **Action**: Have a Yamaha dealer replace the fuel filter when necessary.### Fuel Tank InspectionYou can visually check the fuel tank for external issues, but any internal service must be done by a dealer.<PIC>*   **What to Check**: Look at the fuel tank for any signs of leakage or water in the tank.*   **Action**: If you find water in the fuel system or if the fuel tank needs to be cleaned, have a Yamaha dealer service the watercraft.

To adjust the adjustable sponsons on your jet ski:1.  **Remove the bolts** on both sponsons.      <PIC>2.  **Reposition the sponsons.** Remove both sponsons, and then install them in the desired position. Be sure to install both sponsons to the same level.3.  **Tighten the bolts** on both sponsons to the correct torque.      <PIC>      Tightening torque: **18 N·m (1.8 kgf·m, 13 ft·lb)**Make sure both sponsons are securely fastened and set to the identical position before operating.

To clean the jet intake and impeller, you will need to beach the watercraft and remove the debris by hand. Follow these steps carefully:1.  **Beach and secure the watercraft.**      Beach the watercraft and always stop the engine.      <PIC>2.  **Disable the engine.**      Shut the engine off and remove the clip from the engine shut-off switch. This is critical to prevent severe injury from the rotating parts of the jet pump.      **Note:** The engine will not start when the clip is removed.3.  **Turn the watercraft onto its side.**      Turn the watercraft onto its port (left) side as shown. Place a suitable clean cloth or carpeting underneath to protect it from scratches. Support the bow so the handlebars are not bent or damaged.      <PIC>4.  **Remove the debris.**      Remove any weeds or debris from around the drive shaft, impeller, pump housing, and jet thrust nozzle. If the debris is difficult to remove, consult your Yamaha dealer.**Warning:** Do not attempt to run the engine if you suspect the intake or impeller is clogged. Continuing operation can cause the engine to overheat and seize.

To start your jetski, you'll need to understand the basic procedure for a normal start, as well as what to do if the engine is flooded or the battery is dead. Here’s how to handle each situation:### Normal Starting Procedure1.  **Attach the Engine Shut-Off Cord (Lanyard):** Place the clip of the engine shut-off cord on the engine shut-off switch. Always attach the other end of the cord to your personal flotation device (PFD) or wrist so the engine stops if you fall off.2.  **Start the Engine:** While lightly squeezing the throttle lever, press and hold the start switch.    <PIC>3.  **Release the Start Switch:** Release the start switch as soon as the engine begins to run.4.  **Warm Up:** After the engine starts, release the throttle lever to let the engine idle and warm up.### If the Engine is FloodedIf the engine doesn't start after several attempts, it may be flooded with fuel. To clear a flooded engine:1.  **Squeeze and Hold:** Squeeze the throttle lever all the way and hold it in the fully open position.2.  **Crank the Engine:** Crank the engine by pressing the start switch for 5 seconds. The engine will not start with the throttle fully open.3.  **Try a Normal Start:** Release the throttle and try starting the engine normally as described above.### If the Battery is Dead (Engine Won't Turn Over)If the engine won't crank, you can't jump-start it like a car. You need to use a booster battery with the proper lead connections.1.  **Connect Positive Leads:** Connect the positive (+) terminal of the booster battery to the positive (+) terminal of the watercraft's battery.2.  **Connect Negative Leads:** Connect the negative (-) terminal of the booster battery to the negative (-) terminal of the watercraft's battery.3.  **Start and Disconnect:** Start the engine normally, then disconnect the booster battery leads in the reverse order of connection (negative first, then positive).

Here are the basic steps to board your jetski with a passenger in deep water:1.  **Board the watercraft yourself first.** Climb on board as noted in the previous section, and sit astride the seat. Attach the engine shut-off cord to your wrist, and then install the clip to the engine shut-off switch.2.  **Have the passenger board from the rear.** Have the passenger move to the rear of the watercraft.3.  **Passenger gets on board.** Have the passenger get on board and sit on the seat.    <PIC>4.  **Check the passenger's position.** The operator should check to see that the passenger has his or her feet on the floor of the footwell and that he or she is securely holding on to the operator.5.  **Start and accelerate.** Start the engine, look in all directions, and then accelerate to planing speed.    <PIC>**Important Safety Warning:** Severe internal injuries can occur if water is forced into body cavities from being near the jet thrust nozzle. Do not apply throttle until the passenger is seated with his or her feet on the floor of the footwell and is holding on to the operator.

If you're having trouble balancing the watercraft at a standstill, follow these steps:1.  Have your passenger steady the watercraft while you board. Pull yourself up onto the platform into a seating position, then balance the watercraft.    <PIC>2.  Attach the lanyard to your left wrist, then install the clip on the lanyard to the engine shut-off switch. Start the engine and keep it at idle.3.  Have the passenger pull themselves up on the platform, kneel down, and then crawl onto the seat as the watercraft accelerates.    <PIC>4.  Accelerate to planing speed, then reduce the throttle to your desired running speed.    <PIC>Keep in mind that the heavier the total weight of the operator and passengers, the harder it will be to balance. Do not operate the watercraft if the total weight exceeds 160 kg (353 lb), including any cargo.
The maximum load of the watercraft is **160 kg (353 lb)**. This is the total weight of cargo, the operator, and the passenger.  <PIC>

Here’s how to change the engine oil of a lawn mower:**Note:** Dispose of the used oil at a recycling center.1.  Start the engine and let it run for 5 minutes.    *Note: This warms the oil so that it drains better.*2.  Park the machine so that the drain side is slightly lower than the opposite side to ensure that the oil drains completely.3.  Disengage the blade-control switch (PTO) and engage the parking brake.4.  Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.5.  Drain the oil from the engine (Figure 49).    <PIC>6.  Slowly pour approximately 80% of the specified oil into the filler tube and slowly add the additional oil to bring it to the Full mark (Figure 50).    <PIC>7.  Start the engine and drive to a flat area.8.  Check the oil level again.

To remove the filters of the lawn mower:1.  Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake.2.  Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.3.  Release the latches on the air cleaner and pull the air-cleaner cover off the air-cleaner body.    <PIC>4.  Gently slide the primary filter out of the air-cleaner body. Avoid knocking the filter into the side of the body.5.  Remove the safety filter only if you intend to replace it.**Important:** Do not attempt to clean the safety filter. If the safety filter is dirty, the primary filter is damaged and both must be replaced.

Here’s how to replace the mower belt:1. Park the machine on a level surface, disengage the blade-control switch (PTO), and engage the parking brake.  2. Shut off the engine, remove the key, and wait for all moving parts to stop before leaving the operating position.  3. Lower the mower to the 76 mm (3 inches) height of cut.  4. Remove the belt covers (Figure 73).  <PIC>  5. Use a 3/8-inch ratchet in the square hole in the idler arm to remove tension on the idler spring (Figure 74).  6. Remove the belt from the mower-deck pulleys and the clutch pulley.  7. Remove the belt guide on the spring-loaded idler arm (Figure 74).  8. Remove the existing belt.  9. Install the new belt around the mower pulleys and the clutch pulley under the engine (Figure 74).  <PIC>  10. Install the belt guide on the idler arm (Figure 74).  11. The manual does include important safety information related to pressure release:*   It warns not to open the appliance until it has depressurized and all internal pressure has been released.*   It indicates that if the float valve is still up, the appliance is still pressurized, and you should not force it open.*   It refers to "Releasing pressure: Venting methods" for information, but the details of those methods (such as quick release) are not included in the provided text.The manual does show an image of the float valve in the depressurized state, which is when the valve has descended and its top is flush with the lid.<PIC>The float valve and silicone cap work together to seal in pressurized steam and must be installed before use.

To set up the pressure cooking lid, follow these basic steps:1.  **Ensure the correct inner pot is installed.** Always use an authorized stainless-steel inner pot. Do not use the appliance without the removable inner pot installed.2.  **Place food and liquid into the inner pot.** For best results, you can use the multi-functional rack to steam food evenly and prevent it from scorching on the bottom.3.  **Close the lid.** The pressure cooking lid is the lid located at the bottom of the unit.    <PIC>4.  **Select your Smart Program.** The appliance will begin to build pressure.5.  **Follow venting instructions carefully.** When the program finishes, follow your recipe's directions to select the appropriate venting method. **Do not attempt to open the lid while it is still pressurized.** If the float valve is still up or the lid is difficult to open, the appliance is still under pressure, and forcing it open can be hazardous.

The float valve must be installed together with its silicone cap before use. It is not something you actively “set” during cooking—it works automatically to seal in pressurized steam.  Here is how to ensure it is properly placed:  1. **Check the float valve position when the cooker is depressurized.**     The float valve has descended, and its top is flush with the lid.     <PIC>  2. **Confirm the silicone cap is fitted on the float valve.**     The float valve and silicone cap work together to seal in pressurized steam. These parts must be installed before use.  3. **Verify correct installation before starting.**     Do not attempt to operate the cooker without the float valve properly installed.  4. **During cooking, do not touch the float valve.**     It will rise automatically as pressure builds. Once cooking is complete and the cooker has fully depressurized, the float valve will drop back down.  **Safety reminder:** Do not attempt to open the lid while the float valve is still up. If the float valve is up or the lid is difficult to open, the appliance is still pressurized—do not force it open. Refer to the venting methods to release pressure safely.

Here are the common causes and conditions that can lead to poor reception:*   **Weak Signal (Snow):** If your receiver is located in a weak, fringe area of a TV signal, your picture may be marred by small dots. It may be necessary to install a special antenna to improve the picture. <PIC>*   **Ghosts:** These are caused when the TV signal splits and follows two paths. One is the direct path and the other is reflected off tall buildings, hills, or other objects. Changing the direction or position of the antenna may improve reception. <PIC>*   **Ignition Interference:** The picture may flutter, drift, or suffer from black spots or horizontal streaking. This is usually caused by interference from automobile ignition systems, neon lamps, electrical drills, and other electrical appliances. <PIC>*   **Antenna Issues:** Severely weathered antennas and antenna cables can reduce the signal quality. For the best reception, using an outdoor antenna is recommended, but you should inspect it before connection.*   **Broadcast Source Issues:** An old, bad, or illegally recorded tape is being played, or the program wasn't captioned when it was produced, transmitted, or taped.*   **Stereo/SAP Reception:** Stereo or SAP can only be received if the TV station transmits those signals, even if you have selected STEREO or SAP. Mono sound is automatically received if the broadcast is only in Mono.
